title: 5 4 Resonance
body:
5.4 FORCED OSCILLATIONS AND RESONANCE


When simple periodic forcing is added to the mechanical or electrical system studied
earlier, we have to solve an equation like

                               my 00 + cy 0 + ky = F0 sin ωt                          (1)

where, as before, m > 0, c > 0, k > 0.
This models a mechanical system driven by a periodic force with frequency ω. When the
frequency of the periodic force matches the natural frequency of the system, the system
can start to resonate. This is a critical issue when dealing with real-world systems.


We saw in the previous lecture that the method of undetermined coefficient doesn’t always
work. Whenever your natural guess for yp (t) is already a solution of the homogeneous
problem, we need to generate a new trial solution by multiplying by t. If this is also a
solution of the homogeneous problem, we just multiply by t again.


Example 1 Solve the second order differential equation

                                   y 00 + 4y 0 = 12e−4t

and write down the long term steady state solution.




                                            1
                                     F y = A + Be−4t − 3te−4t , y → A as t → ∞ F
To understand what’s going on here we return to the physical system described earlier: a
mass on a spring.

                          FORCED OSCILLATIONS

                                       R = -ky
                                                               V = y’

                                                                    y
               0                                 Fric = -cy’


Recall that this system was governed by the ODE my 00 + cy 0 + ky = 0 where m (mass),
c (damping coefficient) and k (spring constant) are all positive. We now add a periodic
forcing function to the RHS which drives the entire system with an additional periodic
force with an amplitude F0 and a frequency ω. The ODE is then

                               my 00 + cy 0 + ky = F0 sin wt

We will now show that a tiny change to the forcing function can have dramatic conse-
quences to the solution of the ODE.

Example 2 A forced vibrating system is represented by

                                   y 00 + 9y = 60 sin(2t)

where r(t) = sin(2t) is the driving force and y is the displacement from the equilibrium
position. Find a formula for the motion of the system by solving the differential equation.




                                             2
                                         F        y = A cos(3t) + B sin(3t) + 12 sin(2t) F

The first part of the solution is the homogeneous piece, sines and cosines vibrating at the
natural frequency of the system (3). The second part of the solution is the particular
solution, which matches the frequency of the forcing function (2).

Example 3 Solve the differential equation

                                   y 00 + 9y = 60 sin(3t)




                                             3
                                         F     y = A cos(3t) + B sin(3t) − 10t cos(3t) F




   Figure 1: Solutions to y 00 + 9y = F (t) for F (t) = 60 sin(2t) and F (t) = 60 sin(3t).


All we have done is change the forcing frequency from 2 to 3! But this small change
means that the forcing frequency is now the same as the natural frequency. This leads to
resonance of the system (it hums!) and unstable growth of the solution with time.

Resonance is a very important phenomenon in real-world systems. Sometimes it is a good
thing, for example if you want to amplify a radio signal from a distance satellite. But
in engineering problems it can lead to catastrophic failure of buildings or bridges if they
are forced (by an earthquake, for example) at a frequency that matches their natural
frequency of vibration.

                                             4
 Let us now see what happens as the system approaches resonance:

                                    my 00 + cy 0 + ky = F0 sin ωt
where, as before, m > 0, c > 0 and k > 0. We have already discussed the homogeneous
solution (no forcing). We now look for a particular solution of the form

                                      yp = a cos ωt + b sin ωt.
You can show that a = m(ω02 − ω 2 )F0 /∆ and b = ωcF0 /∆ where
                            k                           2
                          ω02 = and ∆ = m2 ω02 − ω 2 + ω 2 c2 .
                            m
Alternately, you can show that the particular solution can be written in the form

                                         yp = Θ cos(ωt − δ)
with

                            F02                     ωc
                             Θ2 =and tan δ =        2
                                                             .
                            ∆                   m(ω0 − ω 2 )
In the case of undamped oscillations (c = 0), the complete solution is
                                                                 F
                         y = A cos ω0 t + B sin ω0 t +                      cos ωt,
                                                            m(ω02 − ω 2 )
                                                                              p
for ω 6= ω0 . This is the sum of free oscillations with natural frequency ω0 = k/m and
forced oscillations with forcing frequency ω. As ω → ω0 , the amplitude of the forced
oscillation gets larger and larger until the solution breaks down at ω0 = ω. In that case
the solution is instead
                                                   F
                                                       t sin ω0 t.
                           y = A cos ω0 t + B sin ω0 t +
                                                  2mω0
Thus, the system responds with a linearly increasing amplitude when it is forced at its
natural frequency ω0 .

In the case of damped oscillations (c > 0), we saw that the solutions of the homogeneous
equation decay to zero as t → ∞. These are called the transients of the system. When
they die out, we are left with the directly forced response

                                         yp = Θ cos(ωt − δ)
with
                                    F0                                           ωc
                   Θ= p                                   and   tan δ =                     .
                          m2 (ω02 − ω 2 )2 + ω 2 c2                         m(ω02 − ω 2 )
So we see that the combination of mass m, damping c, and the restoring force constant k
can be used to decrease or increase the response amplitude Θ compared with the forcing
amplitude F0 . In fact, by carefully tuning these parameters, weak signals can be magnified
(for example, a radio receiver) and potentially dangerous resonances can be cancelled. An
example of the latter is the 660 tonne steel mass damper mounted in the top of the Taipei
101 building, protecting it from typhoons and earthquakes.
